course_description:
Ecology and sampling methods primarily taught over a five-day field trip in the June/July recess to K'gari (Fraser Island). Soil and vegetation assessment in a range of habitats (from coastal sand dunes to bush to rainforests), ecology of various animals (from crabs to frogs to birds and mammals). There are additional costs for the field trip. Preference will be given to students who have taken or are enrolled in BIOL2010, students enrolled in the Ecology and Conservation Biology Major and students requiring this course to fulfill their degree. Other students will be considered if places are available. There is a quota of 54 students attached to the course. The field trip is offered in the June/July break - not the mid-semester break.

All enquiries for permission to enrol to be addressed to environment@enquire.uq.edu.au

Ecology can be variously defined as the study of environments for evolution, the interaction of organisms with their environment and the scientific study of the distribution and abundance of organisms. As such, the discipline encompasses evolution, development, reproduction, physiology, and behaviour, but at heart, ecologists like to be in the field, undertaking observations and experiments on their system of interest. BIOL2015 is a field-based course that will introduce many facets of the methodology and practice of ecology in a unique Australian environment. Students will undertake group modules in plant and animal ecology, be familiar with techniques for estimating the distribution and abundance of organisms, and species-species interactions in a variety of terrestrial and aquatic habitats.

Attendance at the workshops is mandatory as important information concerning activities on the field trip is provided. Failure to attend all workshops, without approval, may result in a failing grade for the course. The course has a mandatory field trip component. This is 5 days to K'gari Island from 22-26 June . The fieldtrip incurs an additional cost of $600-$700. If you cannot make this trip, you cannot take the course and will need to withdraw. There is always a chance that the field trip will be cancelled, in which caseﾠstudents will be provided alternative learning resources and assessments.

The final paper is due two weeks after the trip ends (mid-July). You will not receive a final mark for this course until August; therefore, you cannot take this course and graduate in Semester 1.

timetable:
The timetable for this course is available on the
UQ Public Timetable
.
Additional timetable information
Theﾠlectures before the field trip will introduce the ecological background to Fraser Island and the group projects you will be undertaking. The workshops will give hands-on experience in handling and presenting the data collected based on previous experience. The post-field trip "tutorial" will assist with further analysing data and writing up the project report.
course_aims: The course will give you hands on experience ofﾠ the ecological complexity of a number of habitat types: from sand dunes, to rainforest and mangroves.ﾠ You will become familiar with some standard approaches and methods in ecology, as well as formulating and testing hypotheses in a field setting, and write a paper in standard science format based on your analysis of a large class data set. The course will initiate closer interaction and collaboration amongst staff and students.

learning_outcomes.description: Appreciate & understand the scope of scientific inquiry in Ecology
Describe ecological processes as they apply to individuals, populations, species interactions, habitats and landscapes
Demonstrate understanding of the variety of ways that organisms interact with both the physical and the biological environment
Use techniques of data acquisition and analysis relevant to ecological questions
Appreciate the natural world through direct experience with local ecosystems as well using techniques for gathering data in the field
Become more proficient in writing a scientific paper in Journal format for publication

assessment_details.title: Field notebook
assessment_details.task_description: The field notebook will record accurate notes of all research done during the field trip and provide answers to questions posed in the Field notebook. The book should be completed on the field trip and the last page will be signed by tutors before you leave the Island. You can retain your notebook till the due date of the report in case you need to refer to the notes for the report write up. Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Submitted to tutors on the last day of the field trip.
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
No extensions are permitted for this task. The field notebook is required to be submitted and completed on the last day of the field trip.

assessment_details.title: Module written as a paper
assessment_details.task_description: Write up the data collected by the class from one of the activity modules (field project undertaken on K'Gari) as a scientific paper for publication. An outline of the report needs to be completed on the Island if you want to receive feedback before the due date. We will discuss in class how to write a paper for publication. Groups of 2-4 will be mandatory. Reports are submitted 2 weeks after the completion of the field trip. Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
The assessment must be submitted through Turnitin on Blackboard.
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
This is a group project with 2 weeks to complete following the field trip. Dysfunctional group dynamics, poor performance by individual group members, or illness or other issues of a group member are generally not considered sufficient grounds for an extension on submission of a group assessment item. These issues should be actively managed by the group and the Course Coordinator as appropriate.

learning_activities.topic: Field trip to Kgari Fraser Island 5-day field trip to Kgari Island with tentative dates between 22-26 June. After the main introductory lecture, an online form to express accommodation preferences (bunkhouse or camping) will be made available. There are limited places available in the bunkhouse, and allocation will be done in chronological order unless there is a valid reason for being allocated a place in the bunkhouse. Delivery: in-person, unless a doctor's note is provided. Learning alternative: complete notebook using video recordings from the field and independent literature reviews. Complete the report using the datasets collected in the course, along with input from group members who attended the field trip in person.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 4 To Week 9 (16 Mar - 03 May)
learning_activities.topic: Lectures L1 Introduction & course logistics; Lecturer: Matthew Luskin L2 Ecological Cascades (Lecture 2); Lecturer: Matthew Luskin L3 Background to K'gari soils; Lecturer: Susanne Schmidt L4 Background to K'gari plant ecology; Lecturer: Susanne Schmidt L5 K'gari invertebrates; Lecturer: Mike Furlong L6 Sampling invertebrates; Lecturer: Mike Furlong L7 Sampling wildlife; Lecturer: Matthew Luskin L8 Estimating wildlife distribution & abundance; Lecturer: Matthew Luskin L9- Species interactions, predator ecology; Lecturer: Matthew Luskin L10- Kgari Indigenous history and modern perspectives; Lecturer Steve Salisbury & guest *Lectures are 1h each, and often there will be 2 lectures per class
Exam The exam is held during the lecture time

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 11 To Week 13 (11 May - 31 May)
learning_activities.topic: Tutorial workshops W1 - Collecting & analysis of plant, soil, and invertebrate data W2 - Collecting and analysing crab data W3 - Collecting and analysing vertebrate data W4 - Visualising data, making interpretations, and scientific writing
